questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Wavelength of an electromagnetic radiation is $\mathbf{4 1 2 . 5 ~ n m}$ From the possible values of given total energy, select the one which will not be transfered by the given electromagnetic radiation. (use $h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} ; \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Electrons present in the ground state in a sample of H -atoms excite themselves by absorbing a photon of $2.04 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}$ energy. What will be the maximum wavelength of emitted photons when electrons return to the ground state (approx.)?
questions.solution:
$\text { Energy absorbed }=\frac{2.04 \times 10^{-18}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=12.75 \mathrm{eV}$
$\therefore$ Excitation is from $\mathbf{n}=1$ to $\mathbf{n}=\mathbf{4}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}=4$ to $\mathrm{n}=3$ on de-excitation will have maximum wavelength
$\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{1240}{1.51-0.85} \mathrm{~nm}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: What will be the ratio of de-Broglie wavelength $\left(\lambda_\alpha: \lambda_{\mathrm{p}}\right)$ when an alpha-particle and a proton are accelerated from rest by a potential difference of V volts and 8 V volts respectively?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lambda=\frac{h}{m V}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \cdot K \cdot E}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q v}} \\ & \frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}=\sqrt{\frac{2 m_p \cdot e \times 8 V}{2 m_{\infty} \times 2 e \times V}}=1\left[\text { As } m_{\infty}=4 m_p\right]\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Consider the following statements :
I-The angular component of the wave function is dependent only on azimuthal quantum number.
II - With the help of magnetic quantum number, Stark effect and Zeeman effect can be explained. III- Orientations of the orbitals in a subshell is decided by azimuthal quantum number.
The incorrect statement(s) is/are
questions.solution:
Angular component of wave function is dependent on I and m .

Magnetic quantum number determines the orientations of orbitals in a subshell.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.solution:
For $5 \rightarrow 2$ transition in $\mathrm{H}-$ atom sample
$\text { Max. spectral lines }=\frac{\Delta n(\Delta n+1)}{2}=\frac{3 \times 4}{2}=6$
$\lambda$ of photon corresponding to $3 \rightarrow 2$ transition : $\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}(1)^2$
$\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right] \therefore \lambda=\frac{36}{5 R}$
$\bar{v}$ of photon corresponding to $4 \rightarrow 3$ transition : $\bar{v}=R(1)^2$
$\left[\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right] \therefore \bar{v}=\frac{7 R}{144}$

For single isolated atom, maximum spectral lines $=3(5 \rightarrow 4$, $4 \rightarrow 3,3 \rightarrow 2$ )
(B) For $5 \rightarrow 1$ transition in $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion sample,

Max. spectral lines $=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=\frac{5 \times 4}{2}=10$
$(\mathrm{H})_{3 \rightarrow 2}=\left(\mathrm{He}^{+}\right)_{6 \rightarrow 4}$ (Not possible for given sample)
$(\mathrm{H})_{4 \rightarrow 3}=\left(\mathrm{He}^{+}\right)_{8 \rightarrow 6}$ (Not possible for given sample)
For single isolated ion, maximum spectral lines $=4(5 \rightarrow 4,4$
$\rightarrow 3,3 \rightarrow 2,2 \rightarrow 1)$
$(\mathrm{C})$ For $7 \rightarrow 3$ transition in H atom sample,
Max. spectral lines $=\frac{4(4+1)}{2}=10$
To emit photon of $\lambda=\frac{36}{5 R}$, transition should be from $3 \rightarrow 2$
(Not possibe)
To emit photon of $\bar{v}=\frac{7 \mathrm{R}}{144}$ transition should be from $4 \rightarrow 3$ (possibe)
For single isolated atom, maximum spectral lines $=4(7 \rightarrow 6$,
$6 \rightarrow 5,5 \rightarrow 4,4 \rightarrow 3$ )
(D) For $7 \rightarrow 4$ transition in $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion sample,

Max. spectral lines $=\frac{3(3+1)}{2}=6$
$(\mathrm{H})_{3 \rightarrow 2}=\left(\mathrm{He}^{+}\right)_{6 \rightarrow 4}$ (possible for given sample)
$\therefore$ photon of $\lambda=\frac{36}{5 R}$, can be emitted
$(\mathrm{H})_{4 \rightarrow 3}=\left(\mathrm{He}^{+}\right)_{8 \rightarrow 6}$ (Not possible for given sample)
For single isolated ion, maximum spectral lines $=3(7 \rightarrow 6,6$
$\rightarrow 5,5 \rightarrow 4)$
Any photon whose energy is 1.55 to 3.1 ev , will be visible.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
